prompts: As project manager, you receive a complaint that your team did not respond properly to a client's request. However, you know the team has been receiving multiple out-of-scope requests from that client. Which action should you take?
A customer complains that the delivery team is not handling requests well. The project manager knows several recent requests are outside the approved scope. What should the project manager do?
A client says the team is being unresponsive, but the project manager sees that many of the client's asks are not part of the agreed scope. Which response is most appropriate?
Your team is criticized for not acting on several client requests. On review, the requests appear to exceed the authorized project scope. What should you do next?

correct.text: Review scope with the client and log extra requests as changes.
correct.why: Scope clarification channels new requests through change control.

correct.text: Clarify the agreed scope and handle additions as change requests.
correct.why: This resolves confusion while preserving formal control of scope changes.

correct.text: Discuss scope boundaries and route new work through change control.
correct.why: Out-of-scope requests need agreement and approval before action.

distractors.text: Coach the team to use five whys with the client.
distractors.trap: Root-cause questioning does not settle scope.

distractors.text: Ask the client to explain requests more clearly.
distractors.trap: Clarity alone does not address authorization.

distractors.text: Include the supplier in all future client communications.
distractors.trap: Supplier involvement does not resolve scope control.

distractors.text: Tell the team to fulfill every client request.
distractors.trap: This bypasses change control.

distractors.text: Escalate the complaint directly to the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalation skips a manageable scope discussion.

distractors.text: Update the risk register with client dissatisfaction.
distractors.trap: Risk logging does not process new work.

explanation: The complaint stems from out-of-scope requests, so the PM should review scope with the client and ensure further requests are documented and processed through change control.

prompts: What should a project manager do to obtain stakeholder agreement?
A project manager needs key stakeholders to align on the project direction. Which activity directly supports obtaining their agreement?
When stakeholder agreement is required before moving forward, which project management activity should the project manager perform?
A project manager is trying to secure agreement from interested parties on project decisions. What should be managed?

correct.text: Manage stakeholder engagement.
correct.why: Agreement is achieved through planned engagement with stakeholders.

correct.text: Carry out stakeholder engagement management.
correct.why: Engagement activities are used to align and obtain stakeholder agreement.

correct.text: Actively manage stakeholder engagement.
correct.why: Managing engagement addresses stakeholder alignment and agreement.

distractors.text: Revise the resource management plan.
distractors.trap: Resource planning does not secure agreement.

distractors.text: Monitor and control project work.
distractors.trap: Performance tracking alone does not align stakeholders.

distractors.text: Schedule a sponsor-only decision meeting.
distractors.trap: Sponsor discussion may exclude needed stakeholders.

distractors.text: Update the cost baseline.
distractors.trap: Cost control is unrelated to agreement.

distractors.text: Perform quantitative risk analysis.
distractors.trap: Risk analysis does not obtain buy-in.

distractors.text: Revise the procurement strategy.
distractors.trap: Procurement planning is not stakeholder alignment.

explanation: Obtaining stakeholder agreement is achieved through managing stakeholder engagement, with the steps already defined in the stakeholder engagement plan.

prompts: An agile project manager in country A works with a development team and business analyst in country B and a QA team in country C. In the second sprint, all test cases fail. The cause is that the business analyst changed the stories' acceptance criteria and communicated the change only to their local team. What should the project manager use going forward to prevent this recurring?

A distributed agile team has developers in one location and testers in another. During a sprint, testing fails because a business analyst changed acceptance criteria and only told nearby team members. What practice should prevent this in future sprints?

On a global agile project, QA discovers that every test case fails after acceptance criteria were quietly revised by the analyst and shared only with a local group. Which practice should the project manager use next?

A remote product team misses alignment when story acceptance criteria are changed in one office but not communicated to testers elsewhere. What should the project manager use to avoid repeating this problem?

correct.text: Use backlog grooming.
correct.why: Grooming aligns the team on backlog items and acceptance criteria.

correct.text: Hold regular backlog refinement sessions.
correct.why: Refinement makes story changes visible and understood by the whole team.

correct.text: Apply backlog grooming with the full team.
correct.why: Shared review prevents local changes from staying hidden.

distractors.text: Password-protect each backlog item.
distractors.trap: Access restriction does not create shared understanding.

distractors.text: Require signed change control documents.
distractors.trap: Heavy sign-off conflicts with agile story refinement.

distractors.text: Enable automated story-change notifications.
distractors.trap: Alerts alone do not ensure alignment.

distractors.text: Move QA into the analyst's local office.
distractors.trap: Relocation is excessive and misses process alignment.

distractors.text: Suspend sprint testing until criteria stabilize.
distractors.trap: Stopping testing does not improve communication.

distractors.text: Ask the analyst to own all test cases.
distractors.trap: Ownership transfer does not align the team.

explanation: Backlog grooming is a recurring session where the whole team reviews and refines backlog items, keeping everyone aligned and preventing uncommunicated changes like altered acceptance criteria.

prompts: You recently took over a project from a project manager who left the company and had an authoritarian style, making all project decisions alone. You notice that a senior supervisor's morale is low. What should you do?
A project manager inherits a team from a former manager who made decisions unilaterally. A senior field lead now appears disengaged and discouraged. What should the new project manager do first?
After taking over a project, you learn that the previous project manager used a command-and-control approach. One experienced supervisor shows low morale. What is the appropriate first response?
A new project manager notices that a senior supervisor is demotivated after working under a prior manager who kept all decision authority. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Identify the cause of the morale issue.
correct.why: Diagnosis comes first so corrective action targets the real problem.

correct.text: Determine what is driving the supervisor's low morale.
correct.why: Understanding the cause prevents acting on assumptions.

correct.text: Explore the root cause of the morale concern.
correct.why: The project manager should understand the issue before changing roles or authority.

distractors.text: Increase the supervisor's project authority.
distractors.trap: It assumes authority is the problem.

distractors.text: Modify the supervisor's role immediately.
distractors.trap: Role changes come before diagnosis.

distractors.text: Speak briefly and update the issue log.
distractors.trap: Logging does not reveal the cause.

distractors.text: Assign the supervisor to mentor junior staff.
distractors.trap: Extra responsibility may worsen morale.

distractors.text: Ask HR to intervene with the supervisor.
distractors.trap: Escalation is premature for initial diagnosis.

distractors.text: Announce a new participative decision process.
distractors.trap: A broad change may not address this person.

explanation: The PM should first gather information to identify the root cause of the low morale before taking any action, since remedies applied blindly are unlikely to help.

prompts: Before a release, two delivery specialists develop a conflict over how to proceed, yet the team must work together to finish the product. What should the project manager do first to defuse the situation?
Two specialists disagree sharply about the best path to complete a release, and their cooperation is needed to finish the product. What should the project manager do first?
Near release, two team members are in conflict about next steps. The project manager needs to reduce tension without disrupting delivery. What is the first action?
A product increment is almost ready, but two delivery specialists are arguing over how to proceed. Before choosing a resolution method, what should the project manager do?

correct.text: Observe their interactions and listen to their concerns.
correct.why: Understanding the conflict first helps select the right resolution technique.

correct.text: Assess the conflict by hearing both specialists first.
correct.why: The project manager should diagnose the situation before intervening.

correct.text: Understand the disagreement before applying a conflict technique.
correct.why: A fitting resolution depends on the conflict's source and dynamics.

distractors.text: Hire an agile coach to teach conflict management.
distractors.trap: Outside help is premature.

distractors.text: Meet with both specialists and HR.
distractors.trap: HR involvement escalates too early.

distractors.text: Tell them they are wasting delivery time.
distractors.trap: Blame increases tension.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to decide the approach.
distractors.trap: Escalation bypasses conflict assessment.

distractors.text: Separate their work until the release ends.
distractors.trap: Avoidance does not resolve shared work conflict.

distractors.text: Apply forcing to maintain the schedule.
distractors.trap: Forcing before diagnosis may damage collaboration.

explanation: The PM must first observe and understand the conflict by hearing both specialists' concerns before selecting and applying an appropriate conflict resolution technique.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: While building a stakeholder engagement assessment matrix, the delivery team learns that a key stakeholder is aware of the project but is unsupportive of its outcome. How should this stakeholder be classified?
A team is assessing stakeholder engagement. One influential stakeholder understands the project and its likely impacts but opposes the expected result. Which classification fits?
During stakeholder analysis, the project manager finds that a department head knows about the initiative but does not support what it will produce. How should this stakeholder be categorized?
A stakeholder engagement matrix shows a stakeholder is informed about the project yet unfavorable toward its outcomes. What engagement category applies?

correct.text: Classify the stakeholder as resistant to the project outcome.
correct.why: Resistant stakeholders know about the project but oppose it or its outcomes.

correct.text: Use the resistant classification for this stakeholder.
correct.why: Awareness combined with opposition matches the resistant category.

correct.text: Mark the stakeholder's engagement level as resistant.
correct.why: The stakeholder is aware and unsupportive, which defines resistant engagement.

distractors.text: Classify the stakeholder as leading the project outcome.
distractors.trap: Leading stakeholders actively support project success.

distractors.text: Use the neutral classification for this stakeholder.
distractors.trap: Neutral means neither supportive nor opposed.

distractors.text: Mark the stakeholder's engagement level as supportive.
distractors.trap: Supportive stakeholders favor the project.

distractors.text: Classify the stakeholder as unaware of the project.
distractors.trap: The stakeholder already knows about the project.

distractors.text: Use accountable as the stakeholder engagement classification.
distractors.trap: Accountable is not an engagement level.

distractors.text: Mark the stakeholder's engagement level as dominant.
distractors.trap: Dominance is not the PMBOK category.

explanation: On the engagement matrix, a stakeholder who is aware of the project but opposes its outcomes is classified as resistant, distinct from unaware, neutral, supportive, and leading.

prompts: A geographically dispersed team has just completed a project. The project manager wants to quickly gather consistent feedback from the whole team so it can be analyzed statistically. Which method should the project manager use?
After project closeout, a project manager needs comparable feedback from every member of a distributed team and wants to analyze the results quantitatively. Which method is best?
A virtual team has finished its work. The project manager wants fast, uniform input from all team members for statistical analysis. What should be used?
Following completion of a global project, the manager needs structured feedback from the full team that can be compared numerically. Which communication method fits?

correct.text: Use questionnaires.
correct.why: Questionnaires collect uniform responses suitable for statistical comparison.

correct.text: Send structured questionnaires.
correct.why: Structured questions provide consistent data across the whole team.

correct.text: Distribute a questionnaire to the team.
correct.why: It gathers comparable input quickly from dispersed participants.

distractors.text: Hold a video conference.
distractors.trap: Live discussion produces less uniform data.

distractors.text: Run a focus group.
distractors.trap: A focus group uses only selected participants.

distractors.text: Request open-ended email replies.
distractors.trap: Free-form replies are hard to compare statistically.

distractors.text: Conduct individual interviews.
distractors.trap: Interviews are slower and less standardized.

distractors.text: Create a lessons-learned workshop.
distractors.trap: Workshops emphasize discussion over uniform data.

distractors.text: Ask managers for team summaries.
distractors.trap: Summaries omit direct feedback from everyone.

explanation: Questionnaires distributed to all members produce consistent, quantifiable responses that can be statistically analyzed, unlike focus groups, calls, or open email.

prompts: A project manager takes over a late, over-budget project delivered predictively with agile iterations for its most critical scope. During assessment, the team appears afraid of making mistakes, seeks individual recognition, and lacks accountability. How should the project manager improve the team's pace?

A troubled hybrid project is behind schedule and over budget. The team avoids risk, competes for personal credit, and shows weak shared ownership. What should the project manager do to improve delivery speed?

After taking over a delayed project, the project manager sees that team members protect themselves, look for individual praise, and do not act collectively accountable. Which action best improves performance?

A project using predictive planning with agile work for critical scope is not moving fast enough. The team seems fearful, individually focused, and disconnected from common outcomes. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Enable collaboration, set shared goals, and reward group results.
correct.why: Shared goals and group rewards strengthen accountability and teamwork.

correct.text: Promote collaboration with common goals and team rewards.
correct.why: Collective recognition reduces silos and improves team ownership.

correct.text: Build collaboration around shared goals and group achievements.
correct.why: The team needs collective accountability rather than individual competition.

distractors.text: Create individual performance plans and publish results.
distractors.trap: Individual ranking reinforces competition.

distractors.text: Track each specialist's output against the next milestone.
distractors.trap: Detailed individual tracking weakens teamwork.

distractors.text: Replace several members to signal urgency.
distractors.trap: Replacement harms morale and misses the cause.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to pressure the team publicly.
distractors.trap: Pressure increases fear of mistakes.

distractors.text: Give bonuses to the highest individual performers.
distractors.trap: Individual rewards deepen silo behavior.

distractors.text: Reduce agile iterations and return to predictive control.
distractors.trap: Method change does not address team behavior.

explanation: The team's fear, individualism, and low accountability call for a collaborative environment built on shared goals and group rewards rather than individual measurement or personnel changes.

eco_task: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
topic: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
prompts: A project manager is delivering new software aimed at a specific niche of university students. To better understand this narrow user group, the project manager needs to develop an archetypal user. How should the project manager do this?
A team is building an app for a small, specialized segment of graduate students. The project manager wants a representative archetype of that user group. What should be created?
For a campus software product, the project manager needs a clear model of the typical end user in a narrow student audience. Which technique should be used?
A project serves a focused group of learners with similar goals. The project manager wants an archetypal user to guide decisions. What is the appropriate approach?

correct.text: Create a persona.
correct.why: A persona represents an archetypal user with shared goals and characteristics.

correct.text: Develop a user persona.
correct.why: Personas model representative users for a defined audience.

correct.text: Build a persona for the target users.
correct.why: A persona captures the typical needs of a user segment.

distractors.text: Contract with an architecture firm.
distractors.trap: Architecture services do not model users.

distractors.text: Send a public questionnaire.
distractors.trap: A broad survey may miss the niche.

distractors.text: Publish a marketing plan.
distractors.trap: Marketing plans promote, not model users.

distractors.text: Create a procurement statement of work.
distractors.trap: Procurement documents define vendor work.

distractors.text: Prepare a stakeholder power grid.
distractors.trap: Power grids classify stakeholders, not users.

distractors.text: Draft a system architecture diagram.
distractors.trap: Architecture diagrams show systems, not archetypes.

explanation: A persona builds an archetypal user representing similar end users and their goals, the appropriate tool for understanding a niche audience.

prompts: At the project kick-off, the project manager announces a hybrid delivery approach. Several delivery specialists raise concerns because they are used to predictive projects. What is the best response?

During kickoff, the team learns that the project will combine predictive planning with agile practices. Some specialists are uncomfortable because their experience is mostly predictive. What should the project manager do?
A project manager introduces a hybrid approach, and several team members question it because they have not worked much with agile principles. What is the best first response?
At the start of a project, delivery specialists resist the planned hybrid approach due to limited agile experience. How should the project manager respond?

correct.text: Educate the specialists on agile principles and benefits.
correct.why: Training addresses concern by building understanding of the chosen approach.

correct.text: Explain agile practices and their benefits to the team.
correct.why: The team needs understanding before resistance can be resolved.

correct.text: Provide agile education for the delivery specialists.
correct.why: Knowledge transfer is the appropriate first response to unfamiliarity.

distractors.text: Escalate resistance to the executive sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalation bypasses team development.

distractors.text: Ask the PMO to enforce the delivery approach.
distractors.trap: Enforcement does not address concerns.

distractors.text: Switch the project to a predictive approach.
distractors.trap: Changing approach before education is premature.

distractors.text: Replace specialists with agile practitioners.
distractors.trap: Replacement is excessive for a training need.

distractors.text: Delay kickoff until the team agrees.
distractors.trap: Delay avoids the knowledge gap.

distractors.text: Document the concern in the issue log.
distractors.trap: Logging alone does not build capability.

explanation: Before escalating or changing approach, the project manager should help the team understand agile and its benefits, resolving the concern directly.

prompts: A project manager takes over a recently authorized project. What plays the key role in determining the appropriate communication method?
A newly assigned project manager must decide how information should be shared with different interested parties. What is the main determinant of the communication method?
After authorization, a project manager begins planning communications. Which factor most directly drives the choice of communication method?
A project manager is selecting communication channels for a new project. What should most influence the method chosen?

correct.text: Stakeholder needs.
correct.why: Communication methods should match what stakeholders need to know and receive.

correct.text: The communication needs of stakeholders.
correct.why: Stakeholder information needs drive channel, format, and frequency.

correct.text: What stakeholders need from communication.
correct.why: Methods are chosen to satisfy stakeholder information requirements.

distractors.text: Frequency of distribution.
distractors.trap: Frequency follows from stakeholder needs.

distractors.text: The sender-receiver model.
distractors.trap: It explains communication, not method selection.

distractors.text: Meeting management methods.
distractors.trap: Meetings are only one possible channel.

distractors.text: The project manager's preferred channel.
distractors.trap: Preferences should not drive stakeholder communication.

distractors.text: Availability of conferencing tools.
distractors.trap: Tools support needs; they do not define them.

distractors.text: The sponsor's reporting template.
distractors.trap: A template is not the primary determinant.

explanation: The project manager must first understand stakeholder needs, which then determine both the communication method and the frequency of distribution.

prompts: A project manager leads a cross-functional team spanning engineering, marketing, and sales. A conflict arises: engineering is focused on delivering on time and within budget, while marketing is focused on meeting the target market's needs. What is the best response?
On a cross-functional product team, engineering emphasizes schedule and budget while marketing emphasizes customer fit. The disagreement is blocking progress. What should the project manager do?
A team conflict emerges between a technical group focused on delivery constraints and a marketing group focused on market needs. Which response best resolves the conflict?
Engineering wants to protect time and cost targets, while marketing wants changes to better satisfy the target audience. What should the project manager encourage?

correct.text: Have the groups collaborate on a solution satisfying both priorities.
correct.why: Collaboration integrates competing goals without sacrificing either side's core concern.

correct.text: Facilitate joint problem solving between engineering and marketing.
correct.why: Collaborative resolution seeks a win-win outcome for both objectives.

correct.text: Ask both teams to work together on a mutually acceptable solution.
correct.why: Working together addresses schedule, cost, and market needs.

distractors.text: Ask each group to give up something.
distractors.trap: Compromise may sacrifice critical goals.

distractors.text: Rule in favor of engineering's constraints.
distractors.trap: This ignores market fit.

distractors.text: Let the groups compete for the best answer.
distractors.trap: Competition deepens division.

distractors.text: Ask marketing to defer needs until later.
distractors.trap: Avoidance sacrifices customer value.

distractors.text: Escalate the decision to sales leadership.
distractors.trap: Escalation bypasses collaborative resolution.

distractors.text: Split the project into separate team plans.
distractors.trap: Separate plans do not resolve shared goals.

explanation: Collaboration is the most effective approach for conflicting priorities, letting both teams craft a solution that meets both sets of needs.

prompts: At the completion of a project, the customer and the organization cannot reach agreement through negotiation. What is the most appropriate next step?
A project is complete, but the customer and performing organization remain in dispute after negotiation fails. What should be considered next?
At project closeout, negotiation does not resolve a disagreement between the buyer and seller. Which next step is most appropriate?
The work is finished, but the customer and organization cannot settle a final disagreement through negotiation. What should the project manager explore?

correct.text: Explore arbitration.
correct.why: Arbitration uses a neutral third party to resolve disputes without immediate litigation.

correct.text: Consider arbitration as the next dispute step.
correct.why: After negotiation fails, arbitration can resolve the issue outside court.

correct.text: Move the dispute toward arbitration.
correct.why: A neutral arbitrator can decide when negotiation cannot achieve agreement.

distractors.text: Inform the project delivery specialists.
distractors.trap: Notification does not resolve the dispute.

distractors.text: Initiate litigation because work is complete.
distractors.trap: Litigation is not the next preferred step.

distractors.text: Begin formal project closing immediately.
distractors.trap: Closure needs the dispute addressed.

distractors.text: Ignore the disagreement and archive records.
distractors.trap: Unresolved disputes cannot be ignored.

distractors.text: Ask the team to redo completed work.
distractors.trap: Rework may not address the disagreement.

distractors.text: Request another internal status meeting.
distractors.trap: Internal discussion cannot settle customer dispute.

explanation: When negotiation fails, arbitration by a neutral third party is preferable to litigation, which risks reputation and the customer relationship; the project cannot close unresolved.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A project manager is preparing an organization-wide software replacement that will significantly affect operations. Training is essential so everyone can use the new system effectively, but training resources are limited. What is the most effective action to ensure successful training for all members?

A companywide platform replacement will change daily work for many employees. Training is required, but available trainers and time are limited. What should the project manager do to make training successful?
An enterprise software rollout will affect operations across departments. Users must be trained, yet training capacity is constrained. Which action best ensures the training is delivered effectively?
A project will replace a core system used throughout the organization. Because adoption depends on training and coaching but resources are scarce, what should the project manager do?

correct.text: Make training and coaching a project deliverable.
correct.why: Defined deliverables receive planning, ownership, and priority.

correct.text: Build a training and coaching component into the project.
correct.why: Embedding it in scope ensures it is planned, not treated as extra work.

correct.text: Include training and coaching as planned deliverables.
correct.why: Formal deliverables help secure resources and schedule for adoption.

distractors.text: Determine each user group's specific needs.
distractors.trap: Needs analysis alone does not ensure delivery.

distractors.text: Recruit additional trainers across the organization.
distractors.trap: More trainers may not be feasible.

distractors.text: Prioritize only the most essential features.
distractors.trap: Content priority does not ensure training execution.

distractors.text: Ask managers to train their own departments.
distractors.trap: Delegation may create inconsistent training.

distractors.text: Publish user guides after deployment.
distractors.trap: Guides alone are not training.

distractors.text: Track training risk in the risk register.
distractors.trap: Risk tracking does not deliver training.

explanation: Incorporating training and coaching as a defined project deliverable directly addresses limited resources by ensuring training is planned and prioritized rather than an afterthought.

prompts: During execution, a previously unidentified stakeholder in the customer's organization, who holds strong influence there, requests a change that could affect scope. Which action should come first?
A powerful customer-side stakeholder who was not previously known asks for a scope-impacting change during execution. What should the project manager do first?
During delivery, an influential person from the client organization surfaces and proposes a change with possible scope implications. What is the first appropriate action?
An unregistered stakeholder with significant influence in the customer's business requests a change that may affect scope. Which action comes first?

correct.text: Identify the stakeholder and update the stakeholder register.
correct.why: The stakeholder must be recorded before their engagement and influence can be managed.

correct.text: Add the new stakeholder to the stakeholder register.
correct.why: Registration is the first step in managing a newly discovered stakeholder.

correct.text: Document the new stakeholder in the stakeholder register.
correct.why: A new influential stakeholder must be identified before processing their request.

distractors.text: Update the project management plan after a meeting.
distractors.trap: Plan changes require approved change control.

distractors.text: Submit a change request to the review board.
distractors.trap: It is premature before stakeholder identification.

distractors.text: Meet with all stakeholders to discuss the change.
distractors.trap: Broad discussion comes after registration.

distractors.text: Reject the request because the stakeholder was unknown.
distractors.trap: Unknown stakeholders still require assessment.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to approve the change.
distractors.trap: Approval is premature before analysis.

distractors.text: Update the scope baseline immediately.
distractors.trap: Baseline changes need formal approval.

explanation: Because the stakeholder was not previously identified, the first step is to add them to the stakeholder register, capturing their role and influence so they can be managed before any change request.

prompts: A project manager overseeing multidisciplinary teams notices friction between some delivery specialists during the last project review meeting. What is the most appropriate next step?
During a recent design review, a project manager sees tension growing between specialists from different workstreams. What should the project manager do next?
In a cross-functional delivery team, two specialists show clear disagreement during the latest progress review. What is the best immediate response by the project manager?
A project manager hears repeated friction between technical and business specialists in a team review session. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Assess the issue and apply conflict resolution techniques.
correct.why: The PM must understand the cause before selecting an appropriate resolution technique.

correct.text: Analyze the conflict and use a suitable resolution approach.
correct.why: Diagnosing the conflict first supports a response matched to the situation.

correct.text: Evaluate the disagreement and facilitate conflict resolution.
correct.why: A fact-based assessment enables constructive facilitation instead of assumptions.

distractors.text: Identify the specialists and review their project performance.
distractors.trap: Performance review may miss the relationship issue.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to replace the specialists involved.
distractors.trap: Replacement is premature and escalates unnecessarily.

distractors.text: Document the friction in the executive summary.
distractors.trap: Reporting does not resolve the conflict.

distractors.text: Tell the specialists to avoid discussing the topic.
distractors.trap: Avoidance leaves the underlying conflict unresolved.

distractors.text: Assign the disputed work to another department.
distractors.trap: Moves work without addressing the disagreement.

distractors.text: Warn the team that further conflict will affect appraisals.
distractors.trap: Threats reduce trust and openness.

explanation: The project manager should first understand the root cause of the friction, then apply appropriate conflict resolution techniques rather than reviewing performance, replacing people, or merely reporting.

prompts: On a complex high-priority project, a superficial disagreement arises between the executive sponsor and a team member that could delay the timeline and must be resolved immediately. The team member wants to preserve a good relationship with the sponsor and asks the project manager how to approach it. Which conflict resolution approach should the project manager suggest?

A minor issue between a senior sponsor and an analyst could slow an urgent strategic initiative. The analyst values the relationship and asks the project manager for advice. Which conflict style fits best?

On a visible project, a team member disagrees with the executive sponsor about a low-impact matter, but the delay risk is immediate. The team member wants to maintain rapport. What approach should be recommended?

A sponsor and a specialist have a surface-level disagreement on a time-sensitive project. Because the specialist wants to protect the working relationship, which conflict response should the project manager advise?

correct.text: Use accommodating to yield on the superficial issue.
correct.why: Yielding fits a minor issue when urgency, relationship, and power difference matter most.

correct.text: Apply an accommodating approach to preserve the working relationship.
correct.why: The issue is low importance, urgent, and relationship preservation is the priority.

correct.text: Use smoothing or accommodating for the time-sensitive disagreement.
correct.why: Smoothing supports the relationship when the disagreement is superficial and time-sensitive.

distractors.text: Use collaborating to fully explore both parties' positions.
distractors.trap: Collaboration takes more time than warranted.

distractors.text: Apply compromising so both sides trade equal concessions.
distractors.trap: Compromise assumes balanced give-and-take.

distractors.text: Use forcing to make the sponsor accept the team member's view.
distractors.trap: The team member lacks authority to force the sponsor.

distractors.text: Apply avoiding until the sponsor raises the issue again.
distractors.trap: Avoidance risks delay on an urgent matter.

distractors.text: Escalate the disagreement above the sponsor for resolution.
distractors.trap: Escalation is excessive for a superficial dispute.

distractors.text: Use withdrawing to step away from the sponsor disagreement.
distractors.trap: Withdrawal does not resolve the immediate delay risk.

explanation: With unequal authority, an urgent timeline, a superficial issue, and the wish to maintain the relationship, accommodating is the most suitable approach for the team member.

prompts: A company that has long used predictive methods is trying a few iterative initiatives within its framework. A project manager is appointed to lead the first project in this hybrid environment. How should the project manager build organization-wide awareness of the hybrid approach?

An organization known for waterfall delivery is piloting iterative work inside its existing governance model. A project manager leads the first hybrid effort. What should help create broader understanding of the approach?

A traditional enterprise begins using incremental practices on selected initiatives. The project manager for the first hybrid project wants leaders across the business to understand the change. What should be done?
A firm with a predictive culture starts a hybrid initiative under its current framework. How can the assigned project manager best increase awareness beyond the immediate team?

correct.text: Recommend iterative methodology training for senior managers.
correct.why: Senior manager education builds broad understanding and support for the hybrid approach.

correct.text: Arrange training on iterative practices for senior leadership.
correct.why: Leadership training spreads awareness where organizational support is shaped.

correct.text: Provide senior managers with hybrid and iterative-method training.
correct.why: Educating leaders helps the organization understand why the hybrid model is being used.

distractors.text: Meet with the delivery team to define roles.
distractors.trap: Team roles do not build organization-wide awareness.

distractors.text: Send the sprint schedule to all stakeholders.
distractors.trap: A schedule informs timing, not methodology.

distractors.text: Invite executives to every daily stand-up.
distractors.trap: Ceremony attendance is not structured education.

distractors.text: Update the risk register with hybrid concerns.
distractors.trap: Risk tracking does not create broad awareness.

distractors.text: Ask the team to explain agile during reviews.
distractors.trap: Ad hoc explanations are not organization-wide training.

distractors.text: Publish velocity charts after each iteration.
distractors.trap: Metrics alone do not explain the approach.

explanation: Training senior managers in iterative methods is the most effective way to build organization-wide awareness and buy-in for the hybrid approach.

prompts: A new agile team is formed with members in three countries and five time zones. What should the project manager do first to ensure effective communication among the specialists?
A newly assembled agile team includes specialists across several countries and time zones. What is the first step to support effective communication?
A project manager is starting work with a distributed agile team whose members rarely overlap during the day. What should be done first?
An agile delivery group has just been formed with remote specialists in multiple regions. Which action should the project manager take first to improve communication?

correct.text: Establish guidelines for team communications.
correct.why: Shared communication rules create common expectations before recurring practices begin.

correct.text: Define communication norms for the team.
correct.why: Norms clarify channels, timing, and expectations for distributed collaboration.

correct.text: Set team working agreements for communication.
correct.why: Working agreements give remote members a clear foundation for communication.

distractors.text: Schedule a virtual meeting to introduce everyone.
distractors.trap: Introductions help after norms are set.

distractors.text: Create recurring meetings for every workstream.
distractors.trap: Meetings without guidelines can add noise.

distractors.text: Briefly colocate the specialists at kickoff.
distractors.trap: Colocation is not the first practical step.

distractors.text: Ask each region to choose its own channel.
distractors.trap: Separate channels fragment team communication.

distractors.text: Let communication patterns emerge during delivery.
distractors.trap: Unmanaged patterns cause confusion across time zones.

distractors.text: Escalate all communication issues to the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalation avoids establishing team norms.

explanation: The first step for a dispersed team is establishing communication guidelines so all members share initial expectations; introductions, meetings, and colocation follow.

prompts: A new project manager joins an existing team and learns that strong personalities have led management to step in over team performance issues. What should the new project manager do when first joining?
A project manager inherits a team where personality clashes have previously required leadership intervention. What should the project manager do first?
After joining an ongoing initiative, a project manager hears that team performance problems may be linked to dominant personalities. What is the best initial action?
A new lead is assigned to an established team with a history of tension and management involvement. How should the lead begin?

correct.text: Review velocity records and observe team interactions.
correct.why: Data and observation help the PM understand dynamics before intervening.

correct.text: Examine team performance data and watch how members interact.
correct.why: The PM needs evidence and context before choosing an action.

correct.text: Observe the team and review its delivery trends.
correct.why: Understanding behavior and results first reduces biased or premature decisions.

distractors.text: Implement senior management's conflict plan immediately.
distractors.trap: Acting before understanding the team can backfire.

distractors.text: Follow the most vocal team member's advice.
distractors.trap: One strong voice may not represent the team.

distractors.text: Identify which team members should be removed.
distractors.trap: Removal decisions are premature.

distractors.text: Hold a disciplinary meeting with the whole team.
distractors.trap: Discipline assumes facts not yet established.

distractors.text: Ask management to keep directing team behavior.
distractors.trap: Continues dependency instead of understanding the team.

distractors.text: Rewrite the team charter before meeting members.
distractors.trap: Changes norms without first learning current dynamics.

explanation: A new project manager should first observe the team's interactions and review its performance data to understand the dynamics before taking any corrective action.

prompts: While drafting the project charter, you learn that two key stakeholders are transferring to other departments and no replacements have been identified yet. What is the most appropriate next step?
During charter development, the project manager discovers that important stakeholders are leaving their current roles and successors are unknown. What should happen next?
As the charter is being prepared, two influential stakeholders announce internal transfers, with replacements still pending. What is the project manager's best response?
A project is still being chartered when the team learns that key stakeholder roles may become vacant. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Perform an impact analysis of the potential delays.
correct.why: Assessing effects and risks first guides the right response and communication.

correct.text: Analyze how the stakeholder changes may affect the project.
correct.why: Impact analysis clarifies consequences before escalation or schedule decisions.

correct.text: Evaluate the project impact of the pending stakeholder gaps.
correct.why: The PM needs the likely effect before deciding next actions.

distractors.text: Put the project on hold until replacements are named.
distractors.trap: Stops work before the impact is known.

distractors.text: Ask senior management to resolve the staffing issue.
distractors.trap: Escalates before analysis justifies it.

distractors.text: Seek sponsor backing to continue as planned.
distractors.trap: Assumes continuation is acceptable without evidence.

distractors.text: Remove the departing stakeholders from the charter.
distractors.trap: Administrative cleanup does not assess consequences.

distractors.text: Assign the team to fill the stakeholder roles.
distractors.trap: Confuses delivery roles with stakeholder authority.

distractors.text: Proceed without updating the stakeholder register.
distractors.trap: Ignores a material stakeholder change.

explanation: Assessing the impact of the departures reveals the risks and delays involved, letting the project manager decide the best response and inform the right parties. Escalation or continuation should follow, not precede, that analysis.

prompts: A hybrid team delivers both predictive and iterative work in parallel. The communications plan states that written communication is preferred, but iterative-task problems arise daily and the email backlog is growing unnecessarily complex. What is the best response?
A team is using a hybrid delivery model. Written updates are the default, yet daily issues on iterative work are creating long email threads. What should the project manager do?
On a hybrid project, plan-driven work and sprint work run at the same time. The team relies on email, but sprint blockers are becoming harder to resolve. What is the best adjustment?
A communications plan favors written messages, but the iterative portion of a hybrid project is slowed by frequent email exchanges. How should the project manager respond?

correct.text: Adopt face-to-face conversations during the sprints.
correct.why: Direct conversation reduces friction and fits fast-moving iterative work.

correct.text: Use direct conversations for sprint-related issues.
correct.why: Agile communication favors quick discussion when written threads slow resolution.

correct.text: Shift sprint problem-solving to live discussions.
correct.why: Live dialogue helps clear daily issues without expanding the email backlog.

distractors.text: Adopt escalated written communications during the sprints.
distractors.trap: Adds more of the medium causing delay.

distractors.text: Use informal chat without any team norms.
distractors.trap: Undefined chat can increase noise.

distractors.text: Adopt formal reporting for each sprint issue.
distractors.trap: Adds bureaucracy to fast-moving work.

distractors.text: Require all issues to wait for status meetings.
distractors.trap: Delays resolution of daily blockers.

distractors.text: Route sprint questions through the project sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalates routine delivery coordination.

distractors.text: Keep email as the only approved communication channel.
distractors.trap: Preserves the problem instead of removing friction.

explanation: For the agile portion, the servant-leader PM should remove unnecessary obstacles; agile prioritizes face-to-face conversation, which resolves daily iterative issues faster than the written channel.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: While forming a team for a global project with incremental deliveries, the project leader shares the performance measurement plan. The delivery specialists dislike it, saying it is not realistic. What should the project leader do to secure the team's engagement with the plan?
A project leader introduces a performance measurement plan to a new global team using incremental delivery. Team members say the plan does not feel practical. What should increase their engagement?
During team formation for an international project, specialists push back on the proposed performance measures as unrealistic. How should the project leader build real buy-in?
A distributed delivery team questions a performance plan during startup, saying the measures will not reflect their work. What should the project leader do?

correct.text: Add self-assessments at the start, middle, and end.
correct.why: Self-assessment increases ownership, accountability, and engagement with the plan.

correct.text: Include periodic team self-assessments in the plan.
correct.why: Giving the team a reflective role improves autonomy and commitment.

correct.text: Build self-assessment checkpoints into performance measurement.
correct.why: Checkpoints let the team participate in evaluating its own performance.

distractors.text: Tell the specialists to follow the plan or risk poor appraisals.
distractors.trap: Coercion weakens genuine engagement.

distractors.text: Ask HR to present the company's measurement approach.
distractors.trap: HR presentation does not create team ownership.

distractors.text: Meet each member privately to sell the plan.
distractors.trap: Persuasion does not give the team ownership.

distractors.text: Remove team feedback from the measurement process.
distractors.trap: Exclusion further reduces buy-in.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to approve the plan unchanged.
distractors.trap: Approval does not address engagement.

distractors.text: Delay measurement until the final release.
distractors.trap: Avoids the issue instead of building commitment.

explanation: Involving the team through self-assessments increases their accountability and autonomy, which drives real engagement far more than coercion, delegation to HR, or one-way persuasion.

prompts: A company moving from predictive to agile finishes the second release of a project. The customer representative is unhappy with the prototype and wants to escalate to management and request a new team. The delivery specialists start blaming each other and the new approach. How should you resolve the team's differences?

After a second release in an agile transition, the customer representative rejects the prototype and threatens escalation. Team members blame one another and the new delivery model. What should the project manager do?

A team shifting from predictive practices completes another release, but the customer is dissatisfied and asks for different people. The team begins arguing about causes. How should the conflict be addressed?
In an organization adopting agile, a release review ends with customer dissatisfaction and internal blame among specialists. What is the best way to resolve the team's differences?

correct.text: Use a retrospective to let the team identify the cause.
correct.why: A retrospective helps the team inspect events and agree on improvements.

correct.text: Facilitate a retrospective focused on the dispute's causes.
correct.why: The team should examine what happened and define corrective actions together.

correct.text: Hold a retrospective so the team can resolve the issue.
correct.why: Retrospectives support team-led learning, accountability, and conflict resolution.

distractors.text: Follow the conflict process in the project charter.
distractors.trap: Charters typically do not define conflict procedures.

distractors.text: Revisit the project vision with the team.
distractors.trap: Vision alignment does not resolve blame.

distractors.text: Review the definition of done with the customer.
distractors.trap: Too narrow for a team conflict.

distractors.text: Replace the team members named by the customer.
distractors.trap: Replacement is premature and punitive.

distractors.text: Ask management to decide who caused the issue.
distractors.trap: External judgment undermines team ownership.

distractors.text: Suspend agile practices until the conflict ends.
distractors.trap: Blames the method without inspecting causes.

explanation: In a retrospective the team reviews the iteration and determines improvements, letting members surface and resolve the cause of their conflict; the other options are misplaced or too narrow.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A team has just reached a milestone successfully, and the project manager notices the team performed exceptionally well. How should the project manager communicate with the team about this performance?
After a successful delivery milestone, the project manager sees that the team exceeded expectations. What is the best way to communicate about the performance?
A project team completes a key checkpoint with strong results. How should the project manager respond to the team's performance?
The team has just delivered a major outcome successfully and demonstrated excellent collaboration. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Give the team feedback on their strong performance soon.
correct.why: Timely positive feedback reinforces desired behavior.

correct.text: Recognize the team's good performance at the next meeting.
correct.why: Prompt recognition helps sustain morale and effective behaviors.

correct.text: Provide positive feedback while the achievement is fresh.
correct.why: Feedback is most useful when close to the observed performance.

distractors.text: Say nothing because milestone delivery is expected.
distractors.trap: Misses a chance to reinforce good work.

distractors.text: Wait until project close to discuss performance.
distractors.trap: Delayed feedback loses impact.

distractors.text: Focus only on the remaining project work.
distractors.trap: Ignores useful recognition.

distractors.text: Document the success without telling the team.
distractors.trap: Records performance but gives no feedback.

distractors.text: Reserve recognition for individual annual reviews.
distractors.trap: Too late and not team-focused.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to mention it at closure.
distractors.trap: Unnecessarily delays and delegates feedback.

explanation: The PM should give performance feedback as often as possible so good work is reinforced and issues corrected; delivering it at the next meeting avoids the delay or silence the other options imply.

prompts: At the start of an agile project, the project manager is developing the charter with the team, but the members cannot agree on the team values. Which topic should the project manager discuss with them to resolve this?
During agile team chartering, members disagree about the values they should adopt. Which topic would best support that discussion?
An agile team is creating its charter and cannot align on shared values. What subject should the project manager raise?
While establishing a team charter, an agile group debates which values should guide their work. Which topic is most relevant?

correct.text: Sustainable pace.
correct.why: Sustainable pace is a team value commonly captured in agile charters.

correct.text: Maintaining a sustainable work pace.
correct.why: Pace and core working expectations belong in team values.

correct.text: A sustainable pace for the team.
correct.why: It directly addresses shared working values in the charter.

distractors.text: Definition of done.
distractors.trap: A completion standard, not a team value.

distractors.text: Respecting the timebox.
distractors.trap: Ceremony discipline, not a foundational value.

distractors.text: Project ceremonies.
distractors.trap: Events are process mechanics.

distractors.text: Release burnup reporting.
distractors.trap: A tracking method, not a value.

distractors.text: Backlog refinement frequency.
distractors.trap: A planning practice, not a team value.

distractors.text: Acceptance criteria format.
distractors.trap: Defines work quality, not shared values.

explanation: Team values such as sustainable pace and core hours are what the charter's values discussion should cover; the other options are process practices, not values.

prompts: On a multidisciplinary project, a project manager is delegating some project management activities to several delivery specialists. Which action should come first?
A project manager plans to assign selected project management tasks to specialists on a cross-functional team. What should be done before delegation?
Several delivery specialists will take on portions of project management work. What is the project manager's first step?
In a multidisciplinary team, the project manager wants to distribute management activities among team members. Which action should happen first?

correct.text: Create a RACI chart.
correct.why: Roles and responsibility ownership must be clear before delegation.

correct.text: Define responsibilities with a RACI matrix.
correct.why: A RACI clarifies who is responsible, accountable, consulted, and informed.

correct.text: Prepare a responsibility assignment matrix.
correct.why: Delegation works only after accountability and participation are defined.

distractors.text: Create and distribute a stakeholder communications plan.
distractors.trap: Communication planning follows role clarity.

distractors.text: Create a mentoring plan for the specialists.
distractors.trap: Helpful later, but not prerequisite role clarity.

distractors.text: Create an OBS and a WBS.
distractors.trap: Structures work, not delegated ownership.

distractors.text: Ask each specialist to choose preferred activities.
distractors.trap: Preference does not establish accountability.

distractors.text: Update the issue log with delegation risks.
distractors.trap: Risk tracking does not assign responsibility.

distractors.text: Tell the team the sponsor approved delegation.
distractors.trap: Approval does not clarify who does what.

explanation: Before delegating, the PM must clearly define each person's roles and responsibilities via a RACI chart; the communications plan, mentoring plan, and OBS/WBS all follow from that clarity.

prompts: You are assigned to an important project whose stakeholders hold different expectations, and some have a high degree of influence. How should you handle this situation?
A project has stakeholders with conflicting expectations, and several can strongly affect outcomes. What should the project manager do?
On a strategic initiative, stakeholder needs vary widely and influence levels are uneven. How should the project manager approach alignment?
A project manager discovers that powerful stakeholders expect different things from the project. What is the best way to handle this?

correct.text: Elicit and negotiate stakeholder needs according to influence.
correct.why: Influence and interest guide how needs are understood, prioritized, and negotiated.

correct.text: Assess influence and negotiate stakeholder expectations.
correct.why: Stakeholder analysis supports balanced negotiation toward project goals.

correct.text: Work with stakeholders to negotiate needs based on influence.
correct.why: The PM should align expectations while considering each stakeholder's power.

distractors.text: Control stakeholder influence to fit project objectives.
distractors.trap: The PM cannot control stakeholder power.

distractors.text: Adopt the most influential stakeholders' needs first.
distractors.trap: Favors power instead of balanced negotiation.

distractors.text: Apply every expectation without affecting objectives.
distractors.trap: Assumes all expectations can coexist.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to select the winning expectations.
distractors.trap: Avoids PM facilitation and negotiation.

distractors.text: Ignore low-influence stakeholders until execution.
distractors.trap: Low influence does not mean irrelevant.

distractors.text: Document expectations and defer alignment to closure.
distractors.trap: Alignment is needed early, not at closure.

explanation: The PM must first understand each stakeholder's influence and interest, then negotiate and align their needs with the project's goals; the alternatives assume unrealistic control or skip that assessment.

prompts: On a hybrid project, the customer does not understand the need for their ongoing involvement, and as a result rework has climbed beyond acceptable levels. What should the project manager do to address this?
A hybrid team is seeing excessive rework because the customer rarely participates and does not see why involvement matters. What should the project manager do?
Customer availability is low on a hybrid initiative, and repeated rework is increasing. The customer does not understand the value of engagement. What is the best response?
On a project using predictive and iterative practices, rework is rising because the customer is not engaged. How should the project manager address the root cause?

correct.text: Meet with the customer to stress how engagement drives success.
correct.why: A direct conversation addresses the root cause: lack of customer commitment.

correct.text: Discuss with the customer why ongoing involvement is needed.
correct.why: Clarifying the customer's role helps reduce rework caused by disengagement.

correct.text: Work with the customer to gain commitment to participate.
correct.why: Customer commitment is essential for feedback and alignment in hybrid work.

distractors.text: Submit a change request for customer involvement.
distractors.trap: Engagement is not secured through change control.

distractors.text: Add the customer issue to the product backlog.
distractors.trap: Queues the problem instead of addressing it.

distractors.text: Ask senior management to compel the customer.
distractors.trap: Escalates before direct engagement.

distractors.text: Continue delivery and absorb the rework.
distractors.trap: Leaves the root cause untouched.

distractors.text: Replace customer reviews with internal approvals.
distractors.trap: Removes the feedback source needed.

distractors.text: Reduce customer communication to milestone reports.
distractors.trap: Less involvement can increase rework.

explanation: Directly engaging the customer to gain commitment to their involvement, which is vital in a hybrid project, addresses the cause of the excess rework; a change request, backlog entry, or escalation do not.

prompts: While gathering requirements for the project scope, the project manager finds that some stakeholder requirements conflict. What should the project manager do to define a clear scope?
During scope definition, stakeholders provide requirements that cannot all be satisfied as stated. What should the project manager do?
A project manager discovers conflicting stakeholder requirements while eliciting scope needs. What is the best way to move toward clear scope?
While collecting project requirements, the project manager notices disagreements among affected stakeholders. What should be done?

correct.text: Help the affected stakeholders reach a consensus.
correct.why: Consensus among affected parties creates a shared, clear scope.

correct.text: Facilitate agreement among the affected stakeholders.
correct.why: The PM should guide stakeholders toward an agreed requirement set.

correct.text: Work with stakeholders to reconcile the conflicting needs.
correct.why: Reconciling conflicts collaboratively supports scope clarity and buy-in.

distractors.text: Follow the technical consultant's recommendation.
distractors.trap: One expert should not decide stakeholder tradeoffs alone.

distractors.text: Escalate to the executive sponsor for a decision.
distractors.trap: Escalates before facilitation.

distractors.text: Meet only with the disagreeing parties.
distractors.trap: May exclude other affected stakeholders.

distractors.text: Select the lowest-cost requirement set.
distractors.trap: Cost alone does not define valid scope.

distractors.text: Document all conflicts and continue planning.
distractors.trap: Records the issue without resolving scope.

distractors.text: Prioritize requirements by stakeholder seniority.
distractors.trap: Seniority is not collaborative consensus.

explanation: The PM must proactively bring the affected stakeholders together to reach consensus so the scope is clear; deferring to a consultant, escalating, or meeting only some parties fails to reconcile all needs.

prompts: A team member hired for six months to complete a specific task decides to leave in the fifth month for another job. What should the project manager's immediate action be?
A contracted specialist engaged for a fixed period announces they will leave before finishing the assignment. What should the project manager do first?
Near the end of a time-bound contract, a team member says they are taking another role and will depart early. What is the immediate project manager action?
A project contributor hired for a specific task plans to exit before the agreed term is complete. How should the project manager respond first?

correct.text: Confirm the contract terms for an orderly closeout.
correct.why: Notice obligations and deliverables define the immediate transition options.

correct.text: Review the contract terms before taking further action.
correct.why: The PM must understand rights, duties, and remaining obligations first.

correct.text: Check the agreement terms for the departing member.
correct.why: Contract terms guide closeout, handoff, and any required notice.

distractors.text: Amend the contract to require completion this month.
distractors.trap: May be impractical or unenforceable.

distractors.text: Identify affected work and secure more resources.
distractors.trap: Important after contractual obligations are understood.

distractors.text: Use contingency funds to hire a replacement.
distractors.trap: A later step after assessing obligations.

distractors.text: Escalate immediately to the sponsor for removal.
distractors.trap: Escalates before checking the agreement.

distractors.text: Reassign all remaining tasks to the team.
distractors.trap: Acts before understanding handoff requirements.

distractors.text: Mark the specialist as unavailable in the schedule.
distractors.trap: Administrative update is not the immediate action.

explanation: The PM should first confirm the contract terms, such as notice period and outstanding deliverables, to plan a smooth transition; securing resources or checking contingency follows that understanding.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: A five-year highway upgrade is in its third year when a local community group complains that the highway's realigned path now runs closer to their homes. The dispute has halted construction and endangered the project. What should the project manager have done to prevent this?
Midway through a long infrastructure project, residents object that the new alignment affects their neighborhood more than expected, stopping work. What should have prevented the conflict?
A public works project is disrupted when nearby residents learn late that a route change brings construction closer to their homes. What should the project manager have done earlier?
On a multi-year transport project, community opposition to a changed path halts construction. Which preventive action should the project manager have taken?

correct.text: Held community meetings about progress and changes.
correct.why: Transparent external engagement surfaces concerns before they escalate.

correct.text: Kept community stakeholders informed through regular meetings.
correct.why: Open communication builds awareness and trust with affected stakeholders.

correct.text: Engaged the local community regularly about project changes.
correct.why: Proactive engagement helps identify and address concerns early.

distractors.text: Created a contingency plan for community opposition.
distractors.trap: Reactive planning does not prevent mistrust.

distractors.text: Established an internal quality review board.
distractors.trap: Quality review misses stakeholder concerns.

distractors.text: Kept project planning confidential and documented.
distractors.trap: Secrecy can fuel opposition.

distractors.text: Asked regulators to approve the alignment faster.
distractors.trap: Permits do not replace stakeholder engagement.

distractors.text: Limited communications to official press releases.
distractors.trap: One-way notices are weaker than engagement.

distractors.text: Delayed community contact until construction impacts appeared.
distractors.trap: Waiting allows concerns to escalate.

explanation: Transparent planning that engages external stakeholders is essential; regular community meetings would have surfaced the routing concern and built trust, preventing the conflict, unlike contingency planning, QA boards, or confidentiality.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: During a retrospective, delivery specialists are surprised by the team performance results the project lead shares, saying they were unaware of the metrics and the tools in place. What should the project lead have done to prevent this?
At a team review, specialists challenge performance results because they did not know which measures or tools were being used. What should have been done earlier?
A project lead presents performance outcomes, but the delivery team says the criteria were never clear. Which preventive action was missing?
During reflection, team members are surprised by reported performance data and say the measurement approach was unclear. What should the project lead have done?

correct.text: Planned and defined a clear performance measurement framework.
correct.why: A framework establishes metrics and tools before results are reported.

correct.text: Define the performance measurement framework up front.
correct.why: Clear upfront measures prevent surprise and confusion later.

correct.text: Establish agreed performance metrics and tools early.
correct.why: Shared criteria make performance results transparent and expected.

distractors.text: Ask HR to explain performance measurement tools.
distractors.trap: HR explanation does not define team metrics.

distractors.text: Instruct specialists to submit feedback regularly.
distractors.trap: Feedback flow does not clarify measures.

distractors.text: Give specialists continuous performance feedback.
distractors.trap: Feedback still needs agreed criteria.

distractors.text: Report only positive performance results.
distractors.trap: Selective reporting hides the framework issue.

distractors.text: Wait until retrospective to introduce metrics.
distractors.trap: Too late for transparency.

distractors.text: Let each specialist choose personal metrics.
distractors.trap: Individual choices prevent a shared framework.

explanation: A clear, planned performance measurement framework establishes agreed metrics and tools systematically, so the team understands how they are measured; HR involvement or feedback routines do not fill that gap.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: While forming a team for a global project, the project leader holds an ice-breaker meeting among the diverse delivery specialists. What should the leader do to encourage the team to voice their expectations?
A project leader is bringing together a diverse global team and wants members to express expectations during formation. What should the leader do?
During startup for an international project, the leader wants delivery specialists from different backgrounds to share their expectations. Which approach is best?
A newly formed distributed team is meeting for the first time. What should the project leader use to encourage members to speak about expectations?

correct.text: Begin with a team self-assessment.
correct.why: Self-assessment encourages reflection, ownership, and openness.

correct.text: Use a team self-assessment to start the discussion.
correct.why: It helps members reflect and voice expectations with more autonomy.

correct.text: Facilitate an initial self-assessment with the team.
correct.why: Self-assessment creates a participative way to surface expectations.

distractors.text: Ask specialists to write expectations on sticky notes.
distractors.trap: Collects input but builds less ownership.

distractors.text: Ask each member direct questions in turn.
distractors.trap: Can put diverse members on the spot.

distractors.text: Describe incentives for successful project delivery.
distractors.trap: Rewards do not surface expectations.

distractors.text: Have the sponsor define team expectations.
distractors.trap: Sponsor direction limits team voice.

distractors.text: Send a survey after the ice-breaker meeting.
distractors.trap: Delays the shared team conversation.

distractors.text: Review the project schedule with the team.
distractors.trap: Schedule review does not invite expectations.

explanation: Starting with a team self-assessment makes members reflect on how they perform and increases accountability, encouraging them to express expectations; direct questioning or incentives do not foster the same openness.

prompts: Early in a project, the project manager realizes that a key delivery specialist is not yet prepared to perform an assigned task. How should the project manager handle this?
Soon after work begins, the project manager sees that an important specialist lacks readiness for an assigned activity. What should the project manager do?
A project manager discovers that a team member may not have the skills needed for a current task. What is the best response?
At the start of delivery, a key specialist appears unprepared for work they have been assigned. How should the project manager proceed?

correct.text: Discuss the specialist's training needs directly with them.
correct.why: A direct discussion identifies gaps and supports a targeted development plan.

correct.text: Meet with the specialist to assess needed training.
correct.why: The PM should understand the specific skill gap with the person involved.

correct.text: Review the readiness gap and training needs together.
correct.why: Collaborative discussion leads to practical support for the assigned task.

distractors.text: Ensure training is added to the resource management plan.
distractors.trap: Long-term planning misses the immediate gap.

distractors.text: Walk through each task only as it begins.
distractors.trap: Guidance does not address underlying readiness.

distractors.text: Escalate to the specialist's functional manager.
distractors.trap: Offloads the PM's coaching responsibility.

distractors.text: Replace the specialist with a more experienced person.
distractors.trap: Premature before assessing support needs.

distractors.text: Document the issue for the next performance review.
distractors.trap: Delays help when support is needed now.

distractors.text: Reassign the task without discussing the gap.
distractors.trap: Avoids coaching and may harm trust.

explanation: Identifying the specific skill and knowledge gaps with the team member lets the PM tailor training, mentoring, or shadowing to close them, which the other options fail to do.

prompts: A virtual project team includes members from several countries with different cultural norms. The project manager is concerned these differences could lead to miscommunication and conflict. What should the project manager do to reduce potential conflicts?

A project manager is leading a remote product team with people in multiple regions. Because work habits and cultural expectations vary, the manager wants to prevent misunderstandings before they affect collaboration. What should the project manager do?

Several international specialists are joining a virtual project. The project manager expects that different cultural norms could create confusion about response times, meetings, and teamwork. What action best reduces the risk of conflict?

A distributed project team spans countries with different norms for communication and decision making. Before problems appear, the project manager wants to lower the chance of miscommunication. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Collaboratively define and communicate project-specific ground rules.
correct.why: Shared working agreements align expectations early and reduce preventable misunderstandings.

correct.text: Create team ground rules together and make them visible to everyone.
correct.why: Jointly agreed rules give the team a common way to work across cultures.

correct.text: Facilitate agreement on team operating rules and communicate them.
correct.why: A shared operating model prevents ambiguity about how the team should collaborate.

distractors.text: Require all team communications to be handled only in writing.
distractors.trap: May reduce ambiguity but does not build shared norms.

distractors.text: Assign the senior member from one country to arbitrate disputes.
distractors.trap: Creates a dispute mechanism instead of preventing conflict.

distractors.text: Make conflict decisions yourself and announce them to the team.
distractors.trap: Top-down control weakens shared ownership.

distractors.text: Ask each regional lead to follow their local communication customs.
distractors.trap: Preserves differences without aligning the team.

distractors.text: Escalate cultural concerns to human resources before kickoff.
distractors.trap: Avoids the team-level agreement needed.

distractors.text: Schedule more status meetings across all time zones.
distractors.trap: Adds meetings but not shared behavior expectations.

explanation: Collaboratively defining and communicating ground rules addresses potential points of miscommunication early and keeps a diverse team on the same page, unlike reactive or unilateral measures.

prompts: Two delivery specialists disagree about how to complete a project task. Which conflict-resolution method should the project manager use to create a win-win outcome?
Two team members have opposing ideas for finishing an important work package. The project manager wants both people to support the result. Which conflict-resolution technique fits best?
A pair of analysts are stuck in conflict over the best way to complete assigned work. The project manager wants a solution that addresses both viewpoints. Which method should be used?
Two specialists cannot agree on the approach for a project deliverable. The project manager wants the outcome to satisfy both parties rather than favor one side. Which technique is appropriate?

correct.text: Use collaborate and problem solve to create a win-win result.
correct.why: Collaboration integrates different viewpoints into a solution both parties can accept.

correct.text: Apply collaborating and problem solving to address both viewpoints.
correct.why: This method seeks a win-win result by working through the issue together.

correct.text: Use the collaborate/problem-solve technique for joint resolution.
correct.why: It focuses on jointly resolving the root issue rather than trading concessions.
